### 2026-05-29-1334-epic-unified-installer-single-doctor-surface-agent-first-boo: Epic: Unified Installer + Single Doctor Surface (Agent-First Bootstrap & Upgrade)  `[completed]`

## Summary

This epic delivers a single, reliable, deterministic path for agents (and humans) to bootstrap or update the directive payload and understand the current state of their project.

## Current Problems
- Multiple competing doctor surfaces (`task doctor`, `run doctor`, `task framework:doctor`, `scripts/framework_doctor.py`)
- Fragmented and unreliable upgrade paths for agents
- The Go installer is hostile to non-interactive/agent use
- No clear, single surface that tells an agent “here is exactly what to do next”

## Target State
- The published Go installer becomes the canonical mechanism for installing or updating the directive payload.
- `scripts/doctor.py` becomes the single source of truth for all doctor logic.
- `task doctor` and `run doctor` are thin, consistent shims.
- After the installer runs, `task doctor` is reliably available and gives clear next-step guidance (including when to re-run the installer).
- All legacy doctor surfaces are retired or fully deprecated.

This epic will remain open until the full scope is complete (including documentation and deprecation of old surfaces). Work will be done on a single branch (`epic/unified-installer-doctor`).

## Sub-issues
- [ ] #1335 - Extract `scripts/doctor.py` as the single doctor implementation
- [ ] #1336 - Retire `framework_doctor.py` and legacy doctor surfaces
- [ ] #1337 - Harden Go installer for non-interactive agent use
- [ ] #1338 - Installer – Automatic Taskfile wiring + tool installation
- [ ] #1339 - Installer → Doctor handoff + payload staleness detection
- [ ] #1340 - Documentation, skills, and agent guidance collapse

## Relationship to Existing Issues
This epic is intended to be the single, comprehensive solution to the long-standing fragmentation of installer and doctor surfaces.

The following open issues are expected to be superseded or closed as part of this work:
- #1322, #1321, #1320, #1112, #689, #1050, #1115, #1306 (and related)

## Definition of Done
- Only one doctor implementation exists (`scripts/doctor.py`)
- Agents have a single, documented, reliable path to install or update directive and check project health
- Installer + doctor handoff is deterministic and non-interactive friendly
- All legacy doctor surfaces are removed or clearly deprecated
- Documentation and skills point to the new canonical flow
- All changes follow project testing guidelines (new source files must include corresponding tests that exercise the new behavior)

**Acceptance**:

- #1335 - Extract  as the single doctor implementation `[proposed]`
- #1336 - Retire  and legacy doctor surfaces `[proposed]`
- #1337 - Harden Go installer for non-interactive agent use `[proposed]`
- #1338 - Installer – Automatic Taskfile wiring + tool installation `[proposed]`
- #1339 - Installer → Doctor handoff + payload staleness detection `[proposed]`
- #1340 - Documentation, skills, and agent guidance collapse `[proposed]`

### 2026-06-01-1000-docsagents-wire-refinement-skills-full-trigger-word-set-into: docs(agents): wire refinement skill's full trigger-word set into AGENTS.md Skill Routing (#995 follow-up)  `[completed]`

## Problem

`README.md` and `docs/getting-started.md` (added under #979 / PR #995) document four trigger phrases for the refinement skill's Phase 0 triage workflow:

- `triage`
- `work the cache`
- `pre-ingest`
- `action menu`

These phrases ARE listed inside `skills/deft-directive-refinement/SKILL.md` `triggers:` frontmatter (already present today) and in its Session Model section, so an agent that loads that skill recognises them. **However**, `AGENTS.md` Skill Routing (currently around line 51) only maps:

```
"refinement" / "reprioritize" / "refine" → skills/deft-directive-refinement/SKILL.md
```

A user who reads the new README "Backlog triage" subsection or `docs/getting-started.md` "Working an existing backlog" and types one of the additional phrases (e.g. `triage`, `pre-ingest`) into a fresh agent session may not get automatic routing to the refinement skill from the `AGENTS.md` routing table on its own -- the discovery path goes via the skill's internal triggers, not via the top-level routing table.

This was surfaced by Greptile review on PR #995 (issue 3 of 3).

## Proposed remediation

Update the AGENTS.md Skill Routing table line for `deft-directive-refinement` to add the four additional phrases:

```
- "refinement" / "reprioritize" / "refine" / "triage" / "work the cache" / "pre-ingest" / "action menu" → skills/deft-directive-refinement/SKILL.md — chains to deft-directive-review-cycle at exit
```

This matches the canonical `triggers:` set already in `skills/deft-directive-refinement/SKILL.md` frontmatter and closes the documented-vs-routed gap end users see.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] AGENTS.md Skill Routing entry for `deft-directive-refinement` lists all seven trigger phrases (the existing three plus `triage` / `work the cache` / `pre-ingest` / `action menu`).
- [ ] Wording matches the canonical phrasing in `skills/deft-directive-refinement/SKILL.md` `triggers:` frontmatter.
- [ ] No drift between AGENTS.md routing and the skill's own `triggers:` list.

## Out of scope

- This is purely a routing-table addition. The skill itself already lists these triggers; the skill body, README, getting-started, and commands.md docs are unchanged.
- Not a duplicate of #979 -- #979 was the docs surface; this is the AGENTS.md routing-table follow-up specifically.

## Related

- #979 -- the docs surface that introduced the user-facing trigger words.
- #984 -- the companion webinstaller callout (shipped in the same PR #995).
- PR #995 -- where the gap was first surfaced (Greptile P2 follow-up).
- `skills/deft-directive-refinement/SKILL.md` -- canonical source of the four additional trigger phrases.

**Acceptance**:

- AGENTS.md Skill Routing entry for `deft-directive-refinement` lists all seven trigger phrases (the existing three plus `triage` / `work the cache` / `pre-ingest` / `action menu`). `[proposed]`
- Wording matches the canonical phrasing in `skills/deft-directive-refinement/SKILL.md` `triggers:` frontmatter. `[proposed]`
- No drift between AGENTS.md routing and the skill's own `triggers:` list. `[proposed]`

### 2026-06-01-headless-swarm-launch-engine-task-swarmlaunch-deterministic: Headless swarm launch engine: task swarm:launch deterministic prep CLI  `[completed]`

Provides a deterministic task swarm:launch entry point that turns an operator-supplied, pre-approved cohort into a ready-to-spawn launch manifest. It resolves named stories, enforces the existing task swarm:readiness and #810 preflight gates, and pre-populates the #1378 allocation-context consent token so the monitor can spawn agents without re-running Phase 0 ceremony. This story owns only the launch engine and its Taskfile target, keeping it independently buildable against the frozen C2/C3 contracts.

- Resolve named stories `[pending]`
  - Acceptance: When invoked with --stories 612,547,543,610, the command resolves each issue number to its vbrief/active story file and rejects any unresolved id with a nonzero exit.

  - Acceptance: When any named story has not passed task vbrief:preflight or task swarm:readiness, the command fails with a nonzero exit and names the failing story.

### 2026-06-01-pre-created-worktree-resolver-for-swarm-cohorts: Pre-created worktree resolver for swarm cohorts  `[completed]`

Adds reusable worktree-map resolution so the swarm can accept pre-created worktrees instead of always creating them inside the phased skill flow. It validates each story-to-worktree path against real git worktree state and the configured base branch, creates any missing worktrees idempotently, and emits a normalized mapping the launch engine consumes. Owning a standalone helper script keeps this independently buildable and unit-testable in isolation against the frozen C3 contract.

**Acceptance**:

- Validate mapping `[pending]`
  - Acceptance: When given a story-to-worktree mapping, the resolver validates each path is a git worktree on the configured base branch and rejects mismatches with a nonzero exit.
- Idempotent creation `[pending]`
  - Acceptance: When a mapped worktree does not yet exist, the resolver creates it idempotently from the base branch and records the created path in its output.
- Collision rejection `[pending]`
  - Acceptance: When two stories map to the same worktree path, the resolver fails with a nonzero exit and names the colliding stories.
- Normalized output `[pending]`
  - Acceptance: When validation succeeds, the resolver emits a normalized worktree-map JSON that the launch engine consumes via the frozen C3 schema.

### 2026-06-02-1413-always-include-upgrade-guidance-section-at-the-top-of-direct: Always include upgrade guidance section at the top of directive release notes  `[completed]`

# Always include upgrade guidance section at the top of directive release notes

## Problem

Users on older/stale directive installations frequently struggle to discover the correct upgrade path.

See the pinned issue: #1411 "Stuck on an old Deft version?"

Currently, when we cut a new `v0.x.y` release of the framework:
- The GitHub release notes are populated primarily from the promoted `CHANGELOG.md` section (via `scripts/release.py::_section_for_version` and the `gh release create` step in the release pipeline).
- There is no guaranteed, consistent "Upgrading from an older version" section at the top of the release notes.
- As a result, people who only look at the release they just got (or are considering adopting) often miss the canonical `deft-install --yes --upgrade --repo-root .` command and the associated guidance.

This makes the upgrade experience worse than it needs to be for the exact audience that needs help the most.

## Goal

For every official directive framework release (`deftai/directive`), the GitHub release notes should **always** start with a clear, consistent, and actionable "Upgrading from an older/stale version" section.

This section should be:
- At the very top of the release body (before the CHANGELOG content).
- Short and scannable.
- Link to the canonical guidance (#1411 and/or UPGRADING.md).
- Use the exact recommended command: `deft-install --yes --upgrade --repo-root . --json`

## Proposed Solution

Modify the release process (most likely in `scripts/release.py`, around the `create_github_release` function or the notes assembly logic in the pipeline) so that when releasing the directive framework itself, we prepend a standard banner block to the notes passed to `gh release create`.

Options to consider:
- Hard-code the banner for maintainer-mode releases of `deftai/directive`.
- Make the banner content live in a small template file (e.g. `.github/release-notes/upgrade-banner.md`) so it can be easily updated.
- Only apply this for directive framework releases (not when the release skill is used in consumer mode — see #1317).

We should also decide whether this banner should be injected into the promoted CHANGELOG section itself, or only into the GitHub release body (the latter is probably cleaner).

## Proposed Banner (starting point)

```markdown
## Upgrading from an older version?

If you are on an existing Deft installation and seeing warnings from `task doctor` about skill-path stubs, outdated surfaces, or payload staleness, run the canonical upgrade command:

```bash
deft-install --yes --upgrade --repo-root . --json
```

(Drop `--json` for human-readable output. Download the latest installer from the [latest release](https://github.com/deftai/directive/releases/latest) if needed.)

After upgrading, start a completely new agent session.

Full guidance: #1411
```

## Acceptance Criteria

- Every new directive framework release on GitHub has the upgrade section as the first content in the release notes.
- The section is easy to maintain / update over time.
- Does not affect consumer-mode usage of the release skill (see #1317).
- The change is documented in the release process / `deft-directive-release` skill.

## Related

- #1411 (pinned "Stuck on an old Deft version?" issue)
- #1317 (expanding release skill for consumer mode)
- Recent discussion in #1317 about changelog/release notes handling

---

**Status**: Proposal open for feedback before turning into implementation work.

**Acceptance**:

- Every new directive framework release on GitHub has the upgrade section as the first content in the release notes. `[proposed]`
- The section is easy to maintain / update over time. `[proposed]`
- Does not affect consumer-mode usage of the release skill (see #1317). `[proposed]`
- The change is documented in the release process /  skill. `[proposed]`

### 2026-06-02-1425-installer-upgrade-broken-unsafe-on-vendored-installs: fix(installer): deft-install --upgrade safe + working on vendored (no-.git) webinstaller installs (#1425)  `[completed]`

The canonical upgrade path `deft-install --yes --upgrade --repo-root .` fails and is unsafe on installs produced by the webinstaller, which vendors the directive tarball into `.deft/core/` and deliberately strips `.git/`. UpdateDeft (cmd/deft-install/setup.go) unconditionally runs `git -C <core> fetch/checkout/pull`; with no `.git` in `<core>`, git's upward discovery resolves to the PARENT consumer repository. The repro errors with a misleading `pathspec 'v0.39.0' did not match` -- but had a colliding ref existed, `git checkout` would have mutated the user's project (detached HEAD / changed tree). Fix: classify payload layout (clone|vendored|absent) before any git op, hard-guardrail so a mutating git command never runs against a repo other than the framework payload, and add a git-free file-swap refresh for vendored installs (download release tarball, atomic replace `<core>` with backup+rollback, never carry `.git/`), re-stamping VERSION with the framework source SHA from the tarball (fixes the #1323/#1324 wrong-sha class) and refreshing the AGENTS.md managed section. Emit `payload_layout` + `strategy` in --json and replace the raw git pathspec error with a classified, actionable message.

**Acceptance**:

- deft-install --upgrade on a vendored (no-.git) install succeeds, refreshing .deft/core, VERSION, and AGENTS.md to the target release. `[proposed]`
- Installer never runs a mutating git command against a repo other than the framework payload (regression test: vendored .deft/core nested in a parent repo with a colliding v* ref). `[proposed]`
- --json reports payload_layout and strategy; the legacy pathspec error is replaced with a classified message. `[proposed]`

### 2026-06-02-1433-v0392-vendored-refresh-aborts-extractor-treats-github-tarbal: v0.39.2 vendored refresh aborts: extractor treats GitHub tarball pax_global_header as content root; README still says installer clones  `[completed]`

## Summary

Testing the new **v0.39.2** vendored-refresh path (the #1425 fix) on a real vendored (no-`.git`) install: **detection and the git-safety guardrail now work, but the upgrade aborts during tarball extraction.** Two related defects from the same incompletely-finished work, in one issue:

1. **Bug (Upgrade Blocker):** `extractCoreTarball` mis-identifies the content root as `pax_global_header`, so every vendored refresh fails.
2. **Docs:** the README still says the installer "clones deft" with no mention of the vendored / git-free file-swap path that v0.39.2 now implements.

## 1. Extraction bug -- `cmd/deft-install/upgrade.go` (@ v0.39.2)

### Repro
Vendored install (`.deft/core/` committed into the project, no `.git/` of its own):
```
deft-install --yes --upgrade --repo-root . --json
```
Output (exit 1):
```
Detected a vendored framework payload at ...\.deft\core (no .git of its own).
Refreshing via git-free file swap -- the installer will NOT run git against your project repo ...
Error: vendored refresh: could not extract tarball: tarball content root "pax_global_header" missing after extract
```
Good news: detection (L252) and the git-safety guardrail (L253) are correct, and the atomic swap rolled back cleanly -- `.deft/core` untouched, no `.bak`/temp left. Only extraction fails.

### Root cause
GitHub source tarballs (`GET /repos/{owner}/{repo}/tarball/{ref}`) begin with a global PAX header entry: `hdr.Name == "pax_global_header"`, `hdr.Typeflag == tar.TypeXGlobalHeader` (`'g'`). Go's `tar.Reader.Next()` returns that entry. `extractCoreTarball` captures `rootName` from the **first** entry it sees, *before* inspecting the typeflag:

`cmd/deft-install/upgrade.go` (v0.39.2):
- **L376-378** -- rootName taken from the first entry unconditionally:
  ```go
  if rootName == "" {
      rootName = parts[0]   // == "pax_global_header" for GitHub tarballs
  }
  ```
- The `switch hdr.Typeflag` only skips the header for *extraction* (**L425-428**, `default: continue`) -- too late; `rootName` was already set at L377.
- **L432-438** then computes `contentRoot = destDir/pax_global_header` and `os.Stat` fails -> **L437** `tarball content root %q missing after extract`.

Real content extracts fine under `deftai-directive-<sha>/`, but `rootName` is stuck at `pax_global_header`.

### Fix
Skip PAX global/extended header entries before computing `rootName`. After the `name == ""` check (~L374), add:
```go
if hdr.Typeflag == tar.TypeXGlobalHeader || hdr.Typeflag == tar.TypeXHeader {
    continue
}
```
(Equivalently: only set `rootName` for `TypeDir`/`TypeReg` entries.) The webinstaller's TS extractor (`deftai/webinstaller` `src/lib/bootstrap/emitDeftCore.ts`) is immune because it derives the top level per-entry (`stripTopLevel`) rather than trusting the first entry.

### Test
Add a fixture tarball that begins with a `pax_global_header` (mirrors GitHub's `/tarball` output) and assert `extractCoreTarball` returns the `deftai-directive-<sha>/` root, not the header.

## 2. Docs -- README still says the installer "clones"

README "Getting Started" still describes only the clone model:
> "The installer ... installs git if needed, **clones deft**, wires it into your project's `AGENTS.md` ..."

and the Canonical-path banner frames `deft-install --upgrade` purely as a payload refresh without noting that there are **two on-disk layouts**. As of v0.39.2 the installer also supports a **vendored (no-`.git`) payload** refreshed via git-free file swap. README should document both origins -- binary (clone, with `.git`) vs webinstaller (vendored, no `.git`) -- and that `--upgrade` detects the layout, refreshes accordingly, and never runs git against the consumer repo.

## Related
- #1425 -- vendored-install upgrade support + safety guardrail. This is the direct follow-up: detection + guardrail landed in v0.39.2, but extraction regressed and the docs were not updated.
- #1411 / #1413 -- "stuck on old version" banner + release-notes guidance.
- deftai/webinstaller#217 -- handoff prompt should point agents at the remote README/UPGRADING.

Evidence: tested with `deft-install v0.39.2` on a vendored `.deft/core` (no `.git`); `upgrade.go` read at tag `v0.39.2`.

**Acceptance**:

- Fix extractCoreTarball to skip PAX global/extended header entries (tar.TypeXGlobalHeader/TypeXHeader) before resolving rootName, so the GitHub tarball wrapper dir becomes the content root (#1433) `[running]`
- Add a regression test that builds a tarball beginning with a pax_global_header and asserts extractCoreTarball returns the deftai-directive-<sha> wrapper root, not pax_global_header `[running]`
- Update README to document vendored (git-free, no .git) vs clone install layouts and that deft-install --upgrade detects the layout and never runs git against the consumer repo `[running]`
- Add CHANGELOG [Unreleased] entries for the extractor fix (Fixed) and the README docs change (Changed) `[running]`

### 2026-06-02-doctor-manifest-tolerant-read-issue-1427: v0.39.2 doctor manifest tolerant-read (canonical-first _locate_manifest)  `[completed]`

The install provenance manifest is written to two different paths by two install rails. The Go installer writes the canonical <install>/VERSION (.deft/core/VERSION, #1062); the webinstaller writes .deft/VERSION (omitting install_root). scripts/doctor.py only reads .deft/core/VERSION and legacy deft/VERSION, so a webinstaller-vendored install is invisible to manifest-agreement, install-path-consistency, and the #1339 payload-staleness handoff.

**Acceptance**:

- Add _locate_manifest(project_root, install_root) helper with canonical-first probe ordering (<install_root>/VERSION, .deft/core/VERSION, .deft/VERSION, deft/VERSION) `[running]`
- Use _locate_manifest in _check_manifest_agreement (replaces install_root-only path derivation) `[running]`
- Use _locate_manifest in _check_install_path_consistency (replaces 2-path probe) `[running]`
- Use _locate_manifest in the #1339 payload-staleness read path (adds .deft/VERSION) `[running]`
- Add tests in tests/cli/ for probe ordering and .deft/VERSION detection `[running]`

### 2026-06-02-v0-39-2-installer-vendoring-neutralization-deposit: v0.39.2 Go-installer vendoring + neutralization deposit  `[completed]`

Make the Go installer (cmd/deft-install) vendor the framework as a git-free tarball payload on fresh install and migrate existing git-clone payloads to vendored on --upgrade, then deposit the neutralization (.gitattributes linguist markers + Greptile/CodeQL bot-ignore + CI guard) so consumers treat .deft/core/** as packaged framework assets.

**Acceptance**:

- Slice A: vendor on fresh install + clone->vendored upgrade migration (#1429) `[pending]`
  - Acceptance: Fresh install vendors via tarball (no .git under .deft/core/); --upgrade on a clone layout migrates to vendored via atomic swap with timestamped backup and removes the nested .git; no mutating git runs against the consumer repo.
- Slice B: deposit the neutralization (#1430) `[pending]`
  - Acceptance: EnsureGitattributes deposits .deft/core/** linguist-generated/vendored idempotently; Greptile ignore entry + CodeQL paths-ignore + CI-guard workflow deposited; deposit calls wired into the install flow.
- Orphan: remove .deft/VERSION on upgrade (#1427 Go part) `[pending]`
  - Acceptance: After WriteInstallManifest writes .deft/core/VERSION on an upgrade, an orphaned .deft/VERSION (canonical layout only) is removed if present.

### 2026-06-03-1114-enhancementquick-start-combine-case-g-case-h-into-one-sessio: enhancement(quick-start): combine Case G + Case H into one session for big-jump upgrades  `[completed]`

## Problem

**Reported during a Slizard upgrade from deft v0.18 to v0.29.**

What happens (actual behavior):

QUICK-START.md Step 2 runs its detection in this order: 2b (managed-section staleness, fires Case G) -> 2c (pre-cutover artifacts SPECIFICATION.md / PROJECT.md, fires Case H). On a big-jump upgrade (eleven minor versions in this report's case), AGENTS.md is byte-stale (Case G) AND the consumer project still has un-migrated pre-cutover `.md` files (Case H). Case G fires first, refreshes AGENTS.md, then instructs: "Framework updated. Start a new agent session to pick up the changes." The new agent session then enters Case H and runs `task migrate:vbrief`. Two agent sessions to complete remediation that could have been done in one.

What should happen (expected behavior):

For a stale-AGENTS.md condition that is detected alongside a pre-cutover condition (both true), the QUICK-START flow should:

- run Case G's AGENTS.md refresh (it touches only the managed section -- safe),
- proceed into Case H's migration (the migration re-reads project state at every invocation, so it does not need the new AGENTS.md to be in agent context to function correctly),
- emit a single "Framework updated. Start a new agent session to pick up the changes." message at the end of the combined remediation.

This collapses the two-session friction into one without breaking the underlying contracts (Case G's refresh is byte-deterministic; the migrator re-reads the filesystem; the post-remediation new-session requirement is the same either way).

How to reproduce:

1. Set up a deft v0.18 project that has SPECIFICATION.md + PROJECT.md without the `<!-- deft:deprecated-redirect -->` sentinel.
2. Update the framework to v0.29 in place (or any multi-minor jump past v0.20).
3. Have an agent read QUICK-START.md and follow it.
4. Observe: agent fires Case G, refreshes AGENTS.md, tells the operator to start a new session. New session then fires Case H and runs migration. Two sessions total.

## Root Cause Analysis

QUICK-START's decision tree is structured around "exactly one case wins per session", and Case G's exit prescription explicitly halts the current session ("! Instruct the user: 'Framework updated. Start a new agent session to pick up the changes. The current session has stale context.' Do not continue past this instruction in the current session."). The halt is correct in spirit -- the agent's loaded skills / rules / paths are stale -- but in practice the halt also blocks Case H, which:

- runs `task migrate:vbrief`, a fully out-of-process command,
- re-reads project state at invocation time,
- does not rely on the agent's in-memory rules for correctness.

The conservative single-session-per-case prescription costs a full agent-session round-trip for every big-jump upgrade. The decision tree could safely cascade Case G -> Case H within a single session because Case H's migrator is filesystem-driven, not agent-context-driven.

Contributing factors:

- The decision tree treats every case as a leaf rather than as a node in a remediation graph; there is no syntax for "Case G then Case H".
- The "start a new session" hand-off is the same end-state for both cases, so combining them does not add new restart cost -- it just stops doubling up.
- Big-version jumps are the canonical "both stale and pre-cutover" trigger; small jumps usually have only one of the two conditions present.

## TDD Fix Plan

1. **RED**: Write a test that asserts: given a project with (a) stale AGENTS.md and (b) pre-cutover `.md` artifacts, a single QUICK-START session can execute Case G's refresh AND Case H's migration without an intermediate session restart. Assert that the post-remediation state matches the state produced by running Case G + Case H in separate sessions today (byte-equivalent AGENTS.md, byte-equivalent migrated `vbrief/` lifecycle).
   **GREEN**: Add a "combined remediation" path to QUICK-START's Step 2 / Step 3 that detects the (stale AGENTS.md AND pre-cutover) joint condition and prescribes: refresh AGENTS.md first (Case G's writes), then run migration (Case H's flow), then a single end-of-session new-session instruction.

2. **RED**: Write a contract test that the QUICK-START prose contains explicit guidance for the combined-condition case (either as a top-level Case or as a callout in Case G / Case H pointing at the joint path). The test fails today because the prose only treats the two cases as mutually exclusive.
   **GREEN**: Update QUICK-START.md to document the cascade explicitly. Keep the standalone Case G / Case H prose for the single-condition cases; add a new "Case G+H -- combined remediation" subsection that names the order.

3. **RED**: Write a test that asserts the migrator's `task migrate:vbrief` invocation succeeds (or fails closed with a clear error) when invoked after an AGENTS.md refresh in the same session -- i.e. the migrator does not depend on agent-context state to do its job.
   **GREEN**: This is likely already true; the test is a regression guard so a future change cannot quietly tie the migrator to in-process agent state.

**REFACTOR**: Once the combined path is documented, consider letting Case G's "halt" prescription become conditional on whether Case H was also triggered. Single-condition Case G keeps the halt; joint Case G+H delays the halt until after migration.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Root cause is addressed -- big-jump upgrades complete in one agent session (not two).
- [ ] All RED tests are now GREEN.
- [ ] Existing tests still pass.
- [ ] QUICK-START prose explicitly documents the joint-condition case.
- [ ] `task check` passes.

## Provenance

Surfaced during a Slizard project v0.18 → v0.29 upgrade. The eleven-minor-version jump triggered both Case G and Case H; the round-trip cost a full session restart that the migration itself did not require.

**Acceptance**:

- Root cause is addressed -- big-jump upgrades complete in one agent session (not two). `[proposed]`
- All RED tests are now GREEN. `[proposed]`
- Existing tests still pass. `[proposed]`
- QUICK-START prose explicitly documents the joint-condition case. `[proposed]`
- task check passes. `[proposed]`

### 2026-06-03-1115-docsupgrading-add-big-jump-triage-entry-point-for-multi-vers: docs(UPGRADING): add big-jump triage / entry point for multi-version upgrades  `[completed]`

## Problem

**Reported during a Slizard upgrade from deft v0.18 to v0.29.**

What happens (actual behavior):

`UPGRADING.md` is organised newest-first by version: v0.27.x -> v0.28, drifted-AGENTS.md repair, `deft/` -> `.deft/core/` (v0.27), v0.25.x -> v0.26, triage v1, pre-v0.20 -> v0.20. Each section is internally thorough. But when a consumer is jumping eleven minor versions in one go (v0.18 -> v0.29 in this report's case), there is no triage paragraph at the top telling the operator which sections apply, what order to read them in, or what to skip. The operator has to read every section and infer relevance from version ranges and "Applies when" prose.

What should happen (expected behavior):

`UPGRADING.md` should open with a short "Big jump?" triage section that:

- names the relevant version-range buckets ("pre-v0.20", "v0.20.x to v0.25.x", "v0.25.x to v0.27.x", "v0.27.x onwards"),
- tells the operator which sections to apply and in what order,
- explicitly calls out which sections are auto-handled by `task upgrade` / QUICK-START vs. manual,
- offers an "I am jumping more than N versions, start here" entry point.

How to reproduce:

1. Open `UPGRADING.md` in the repo root with the mindset of an operator on deft v0.18 wanting to land on v0.29.
2. Try to determine which sections apply, in what order, without already knowing the framework's release history.
3. Observe that there is no top-of-file triage; the operator must read every section.

## Root Cause Analysis

`UPGRADING.md` was written incrementally: each release cycle prepends a new section at the top. The doc has no overview / table-of-contents pass, no "applies to N -> M" navigation, and no joint-section guidance for multi-version jumps. The "Future upgrade sections will be prepended here" closing line tells the maintainer's workflow but does not help the consumer triage.

This is a docs-only issue but it has operational cost: every big-jump consumer spends time reading sections that do not apply, and risks running migration / refresh commands out of order. The cost compounds the operational issues surfaced in the sibling Slizard findings -- when each step already takes longer than expected, the doc-navigation cost is the difference between "frustrating" and "blocking".

Contributing factors:

- The doc grows monotonically; there is no shaping pass at release time to surface cross-section guidance.
- Each section is internally self-contained but does not link forward / backward to predecessor / successor sections that a big-jump consumer also needs.
- The version-by-version layout is the right organising principle for incremental upgraders, but big-jump consumers need a different entry surface that the current layout does not provide.

## TDD Fix Plan

1. **RED**: Write a contract test that `UPGRADING.md` contains a top-of-file triage section (heading like "Jumping multiple versions?" or "Big-jump entry point") with explicit guidance for multi-version jumps. The test fails today because no such section exists.
   **GREEN**: Add the triage section at the top of `UPGRADING.md`. Include: (a) a "find your starting version" pointer table or paragraph, (b) the canonical command sequence for a big-jump upgrade (likely "have your agent read QUICK-START.md and follow it" plus the manual fallbacks), (c) explicit links into the version-specific subsections in apply-order.

2. **RED**: Write a contract test that every version-specific section in `UPGRADING.md` opens with an "Applies when" paragraph that names the detectable signal (today some sections have this, some do not). The test enumerates sections and asserts each one has a discoverable applicability rule.
   **GREEN**: Audit each section; add an "Applies when" line where missing. This is mostly already present in the v0.27-onwards sections; older sections may be lighter.

3. **RED**: Write a test that the QUICK-START / AGENTS.md guidance pointers in `UPGRADING.md` align with the actual case detection in QUICK-START.md (no broken cross-references after a future restructure).
   **GREEN**: Add a cross-reference index; this also catches bit-rot from future releases prepending sections that point at moved anchors.

**REFACTOR**: Once the triage entry point exists, consider promoting the most common big-jump path (pre-v0.20 -> latest) to its own runbook in `docs/` and have `UPGRADING.md` link to it; keeps the version-by-version sections lean and the big-jump path single-sourced.

## Acceptance Criteria

- [ ] Root cause is addressed -- `UPGRADING.md` has a discoverable big-jump entry point.
- [ ] An operator new to the project can determine which sections apply to a multi-version jump in under 60 seconds.
- [ ] All RED tests are now GREEN.
- [ ] Existing tests still pass.
- [ ] Cross-references between `UPGRADING.md`, `QUICK-START.md`, and the framework-doctor command surface are mutually consistent.
- [ ] `task check` passes.

## Provenance

Surfaced during a Slizard project v0.18 → v0.29 upgrade. The eleven-minor-version jump required reading every section to determine applicability and order.

**Acceptance**:

- Root cause is addressed -- UPGRADING.md has a discoverable big-jump entry point. `[proposed]`
- An operator new to the project can determine which sections apply to a multi-version jump in under 60 seconds. `[proposed]`
- All RED tests are now GREEN. `[proposed]`
- Existing tests still pass. `[proposed]`
- Cross-references between UPGRADING.md, QUICK-START.md, and the framework-doctor command surface are mutually consistent. `[proposed]`
- task check passes. `[proposed]`

### 2026-06-03-1311-bugscriptsproject-render-task-projectrender-leaks-vbriefproj: bug(scripts/project_render): task project:render leaks vbrief/PROJECT-DEFINITION.vbrief.json.lock; trapped by git add -A on every chore commit  `[completed]`

## Problem

`task project:render` creates a transient mutation-lock file at `vbrief/PROJECT-DEFINITION.vbrief.json.lock` (1 byte) during the render operation. The lock is NOT removed after a clean render. The path is also NOT in `.gitignore`, so `git add -A` traps it on every chore commit cycle.

Observed 2026-05-22 during a deft-directive chore commit (master HEAD `b17d128`):
- After `task project:render`, `git status` showed `Untracked: vbrief/PROJECT-DEFINITION.vbrief.json.lock`
- The lock got picked up by `git add -A` and committed as a tracked binary file
- Detected post-commit; reverted via `git rm --cached` + `.gitignore` append in an amend

The `.gitignore` append landed in `b17d128`, so the maintainer repo is now protected. But:

1. Consumer projects that vendor the framework / use `task project:render` will still hit this on their first chore commit unless their own `.gitignore` includes the path.
2. The lock file itself ideally should not LEAK at all -- `task project:render` should clean up its own lock on successful exit (delete on context-manager exit), so the entry in `.gitignore` is a belt-and-suspenders defense, not the primary fix.

## What landed in `b17d128`

- `vbrief/PROJECT-DEFINITION.vbrief.json.lock` added to `.gitignore`
- Tracked copy removed via `git rm --cached`

## What is still needed

- [ ] **Primary fix**: `scripts/project_render.py` (or wherever the lock is held) MUST delete the lock file on successful exit, not just on context-manager `__exit__`. Verify the lock-acquisition codepath uses a `try/finally` or equivalent that always cleans up.
- [ ] **Consumer propagation**: add `vbrief/PROJECT-DEFINITION.vbrief.json.lock` (or a broader `*.lock` pattern, scope-appropriate) to whichever consumer-template `.gitignore` ships with `task install` / framework bootstrap, so consumers are protected on their first render.
- [ ] **Unit test**: assert that `task project:render` exits with no `.lock` file remaining in `vbrief/` (deterministic gate per the Rule Authority [AXIOM] -- prose tier mitigations should escalate to test-tier once a leak path is confirmed).
- [ ] **Cross-check**: are any other deft scripts using the same lock pattern (`*.lock` files in `vbrief/`)? Audit and apply the same cleanup discipline.

## Acceptance

- `task project:render` leaves NO `.lock` file in `vbrief/` after a successful render.
- The deterministic gate above passes in `task check`.
- Consumer-side `.gitignore` (whichever path that is) carries the matching ignore entry.
- This issue closes when the primary fix + the unit test + the consumer-template update have all landed.

## Cross-references

- `b17d128` -- the maintainer-side `.gitignore` mitigation + the audit-trail commit message describing how the leak was found.
- AGENTS.md `## CHANGELOG entry style (#1242)` -- the commit was discovered during the kind of chore commit the rule governs.

**Acceptance**:

- **Primary fix**:  (or wherever the lock is held) MUST delete the lock file on successful exit, not just on context-manager . Verify the lock-acquisition codepath uses a  or equivalent that always cleans up. `[proposed]`
- **Consumer propagation**: add  (or a broader  pattern, scope-appropriate) to whichever consumer-template  ships with  / framework bootstrap, so consumers are protected on their first render. `[proposed]`
- **Unit test**: assert that  exits with no  file remaining in  (deterministic gate per the Rule Authority [AXIOM] -- prose tier mitigations should escalate to test-tier once a leak path is confirmed). `[proposed]`
- **Cross-check**: are any other deft scripts using the same lock pattern ( files in )? Audit and apply the same cleanup discipline. `[proposed]`

### 2026-06-03-1320-frameworkcheck-updates-probes-consumer-origin-statusreportgi: framework:check-updates probes consumer origin (statusreport.git) on vendored installs; never detects framework drift  `[completed]`

## Summary

`task deft:framework:check-updates` (`cmd_check_updates` in `run`, #801 probe) resolves the wrong upstream URL on **vendored, version-pinned installs** (`.deft/core/` deposit with `.deft/VERSION` pin, no submodule). It points at the consumer project's own `origin` remote instead of the framework upstream (`deftai/directive`), so the gate silently returns `no-tags` and never warns even when the consumer is multiple releases behind.

## Reproduction

Consumer project: `deftai/statusreport` at branch `deftai-upgrade-20260518`.

Install style:

- `./.deft/core/` (vendored framework, no `.git` inside)
- `./.deft/VERSION` pins `v0.31.0` (sha `d4257b4ba8594f06ec8802a1e5c2560d17cb833c`, `fetched_at: 2026-05-18T22:23:29.387Z`, `fetched_by: 'DEFT.directive[bot]'`)
- No `.gitmodules` at the project root
- No `./deft/` legacy submodule directory

Run:

```
task deft:framework:check-updates -- --json --force
```

Returns:

```json
{
  "current": "0.0.0-dev",
  "status": "no-tags",
  "upstream_url": "https://github.com/deftai/statusreport.git"
}
```

`upstream_url` is the consumer project's `origin` (statusreport), not the framework upstream. `current: "0.0.0-dev"` is also suspicious — the consumer's `.deft/VERSION` clearly records `v0.31.0`.

Direct query against the real framework upstream:

```
gh release list --repo deftai/directive --limit 5
# v0.33.0 (latest, ~33 min ago)
# v0.32.1 (~2 days ago)
# v0.32.0 (~2 days ago)
# v0.31.0 (~5 days ago)
# v0.30.0 (~9 days ago)
```

So the consumer is **3 releases behind** but the probe silently reports `no-tags` and exit 0.

## Expected behavior

For a vendored install:

- `upstream_url` should resolve to the framework upstream (`https://github.com/deftai/directive.git`) — most reliably read from `.deft/VERSION`'s provenance manifest, an explicit project-config knob, or a baked-in framework constant.
- `current` should read the framework version from `.deft/VERSION` (or the `.deft-version` marker), not the in-process `VERSION` constant of whatever framework lives on disk (which defaults to `0.0.0-dev` in some build contexts).
- With `current=v0.31.0` and upstream tags `v0.32.0 / v0.32.1 / v0.33.0` present, status should be `behind` with exit code `1`.

## Hypothesis

`cmd_check_updates` calls `_run_remote_probe(project_root)`, which appears to look for a `./deft` submodule and fall back to the consumer project's `origin` when the submodule isn't present. For the v0.20+ canonical install layout (`.deft/core/` + `.deft/VERSION`), this fallback is incorrect — the framework's true upstream is recorded in the YAML provenance manifest at `.deft/core/VERSION`, not derivable from the consumer's `origin` (which by definition is the *consumer's* repo, not the framework's).

## Impact

The periodic remote-drift gate (#801) is effectively dead for vendored installs, which is now the canonical install layout per AGENTS.md template v3. Consumers fall arbitrarily far behind without any session-start warning. The 24-hour throttle and `vbrief/.deft-remote-probe.json` state file are no defense — the probe affirmatively returns `no-tags` rather than erroring.

## Suggested fix

Resolve `upstream_url` in this priority order:

1. Parse the YAML manifest at `<install>/VERSION` for a `source.url` (or similar) field.
2. Read an explicit `framework.upstream_url` knob from `vbrief/PROJECT-DEFINITION.vbrief.json` or `.deft/config.toml`.
3. Use a baked-in framework constant (`DEFT_UPSTREAM_URL = "https://github.com/deftai/directive.git"`).
4. **Never** fall back to the consumer's `origin` — that is guaranteed wrong on a vendored install.

Also resolve `current` by reading `.deft/VERSION` (`ref` or `tag` field) before falling back to the in-process `VERSION`.

## Environment

- Consumer: `deftai/statusreport`, branch `deftai-upgrade-20260518`
- Framework on disk: v0.31.0 (sha `d4257b4`)
- Latest framework: v0.33.0
- OS: Windows 11, pwsh 7.6.1
- `gh` CLI 2.x (authenticated)

## Related

- #801 (remote-version probe)
- #1046 (install-manifest / install-path)
- #1062 (install-root field in manifest)

### 2026-06-03-1323-version-resolver-returns-000-dev-on-vendored-installs-deftco: Version resolver returns 0.0.0-dev on vendored installs (.deft/core/ without .git): every framework surface reports wrong version  `[completed]`

## Summary

The framework's `VERSION = _resolve_version()` (`run` line 50–85, mirrored by `scripts/resolve_version.py`) cannot resolve a real version on **vendored installs** — i.e. the canonical layout the framework itself documents (AGENTS.md v3 template: "Deft is installed in .deft/core/"). On a vendored install with no `.git` directory inside `.deft/core/`, the resolver falls through to `0.0.0-dev` because:

1. `DEFT_RELEASE_VERSION` env var is not set in normal operation (it's only set by `scripts/release.py::run_build` during a release-pipeline build).
2. `git describe --tags --abbrev=0` is run with `cwd=script_dir` where `script_dir = Path(__file__).parent.absolute()` → `.deft/core/`. With no `.git` there, git walks UP and either fails (no parent repo) OR finds the **consumer project's** git repo (which has no framework version tags). Either way the result is empty/non-zero.
3. Fallback returns `"0.0.0-dev"`.

This means every vendored install reports its version as `0.0.0-dev` everywhere the framework's in-process `VERSION` is shown:

- `task deft:install:upgrade` header line ("Deft CLI v0.0.0-dev - Upgrade")
- `.deft-version` marker file (just writes `0.0.0-dev` into `vbrief/.deft-version`)
- `.deft/core/VERSION` YAML manifest (`ref: 'v0.0.0-dev'`, `tag: 'v0.0.0-dev'`)
- `task deft:framework:check-updates` output (`current: "0.0.0-dev"`)
- Any other surface that consumes the `VERSION` constant

## Reproduction

```
# 1. Clone the framework at a real tag into a vendored install:
git clone --depth 1 --branch v0.33.0 https://github.com/deftai/directive.git /tmp/deft-v0.33.0
mkdir -p ./my-project/.deft/core/
cp -R /tmp/deft-v0.33.0/* ./my-project/.deft/core/   # NOTE: excludes .git
rm -rf /tmp/deft-v0.33.0

# 2. Set up the Taskfile include in ./my-project/Taskfile.yml referencing .deft/core/Taskfile.yml.

# 3. Try to use the framework:
cd ./my-project
task deft:install:upgrade
# Output: "Deft CLI v0.0.0-dev - Upgrade"
#         "✓ Recorded framework version 0.0.0-dev in .deft-version."
#         (.deft/core/VERSION shows ref: 'v0.0.0-dev')

# 4. The release-binary path that USED to populate the correct version is unusable for agents (see #1322).
```

## Why this matters

The framework's own AGENTS.md template (v3) declares the canonical install location as `.deft/core/`. The release binary (`deft-install`) deposits there. The `tasks/install.yml` description points consumers at the GitHub Releases binary. Yet:

- The release binary itself stamps the correct version via some mechanism (presumably Go ldflags), but **only into its own Go-compiled binary** — not into the Python `run` script's `VERSION` constant.
- The deposited Python source still resolves `VERSION` at runtime via `_resolve_version()`, which doesn't know about the release-binary's stamped version because no on-disk file records it for Python to read.
- After deposit, `.deft/core/` has NO `.git`, NO baked-in version, and NO source for the resolver to use.

So a fresh install from the release binary will ALSO report `0.0.0-dev` everywhere the Python surface is used, even though the binary "knows" the version. The version stamping is silently inconsistent between Go (binary) and Python (deposited script).

## Expected behavior

The resolver should consult **at least one persistent on-disk source** that survives a vendored deposit. Suggested priority chain (extends the existing one):

1. `DEFT_RELEASE_VERSION` env var (existing) — for release-pipeline builds and explicit overrides.
2. **NEW**: `<script_dir>/VERSION` YAML manifest's `tag` or `ref` field — parse the file the framework itself writes via `_write_install_manifest`. This is the natural place because:
   - It's always present after `task deft:install:upgrade` runs.
   - It already encodes the version (`ref: 'v0.33.0'` / `tag: 'v0.33.0'`).
   - The release binary should populate it during install.
3. **NEW**: `<script_dir>/.deft-version` plaintext marker file — same idea, simpler format. Populated by `_write_version_marker`.
4. `git describe --tags --abbrev=0` with `cwd=script_dir` (existing) — only works for framework-checkout (development) consumers, not vendored installs. Keep this for the framework-self-development case.
5. `0.0.0-dev` (existing fallback).

The release binary should ALSO populate `.deft/core/VERSION` and/or a `.deft/core/.deft-version` file at install time so the deposited Python surface immediately resolves to the correct version without needing the env var.

## Suggested fix shape

In `run::_resolve_version()` (and mirror in `scripts/resolve_version.py`):

```python
def _resolve_version() -> str:
    # 1. env var (existing)
    env_value = os.environ.get("DEFT_RELEASE_VERSION", "").strip()
    if env_value:
        return env_value

    script_dir = Path(__file__).parent.absolute()

    # 2. NEW: read <script_dir>/VERSION YAML manifest 'ref' or 'tag' field
    manifest_path = script_dir / "VERSION"
    if manifest_path.is_file():
        try:
            text = manifest_path.read_text(encoding="utf-8")
            # Match e.g. `tag: 'v0.33.0'` or `ref: 'v0.33.0'`
            m = re.search(r"^(?:tag|ref):\s*['\"]?v?([\d.][\w.-]*)['\"]?\s*$", text, re.M)
            if m:
                return m.group(1)
        except OSError:
            pass

    # 3. NEW: read <script_dir>/.deft-version plaintext
    marker = script_dir / ".deft-version"
    if marker.is_file():
        try:
            value = marker.read_text(encoding="utf-8").strip().lstrip("v")
            if value:
                return value
        except OSError:
            pass

    # 4. git describe (existing) — only useful for framework-self-dev
    # ... existing code ...

    return "0.0.0-dev"
```

And update `cmd_install` / `_write_install_manifest` (the release-binary path) so they ALWAYS write `<script_dir>/VERSION` with the correct `tag` field at deposit time, populated from the binary's stamped version (NOT from the consumer's `git rev-parse HEAD`, which is a related bug — filing separately).

## Environment

- Consumer: `deftai/statusreport`, branch `deftai-upgrade-20260518`
- Install: vendored at `.deft/core/`, copied from `git clone --depth 1 --branch v0.33.0 https://github.com/deftai/directive.git` (no `.git` retained)
- OS: Windows 11, pwsh 7.6.1
- Workaround currently used: `$env:DEFT_RELEASE_VERSION='0.33.0'` before every framework command invocation

## Related

- #1320 (check-updates wrong upstream — same root cause family: no canonical metadata on vendored installs)
- #1322 (release-binary not usable by agents — pushes consumers toward the source-clone path that exposes this bug)
- #1272 (doctor surface consolidation — the new doctor inherits this resolver)
- #723, #741, #771 (existing version-resolution work — this issue extends that line)

### 2026-06-03-1325-version-manifest-moved-from-deftversion-to-deftcoreversion-w: VERSION manifest moved from .deft/VERSION to .deft/core/VERSION without migration; legacy file persists as stale source-of-truth  `[completed]`

## Summary

The canonical install-manifest VERSION file location changed between framework versions without a documented migration path. v0.31.0 consumers had the manifest at `.deft/VERSION` (one level above the install dir); v0.33.0 expects it at `.deft/core/VERSION` (inside the install dir). After an in-place upgrade, the old `.deft/VERSION` is **left behind as a stale, conflicting source-of-truth**, and `framework_doctor.py::manifest-agreement` only inspects the new location — so the stale file goes undetected.

## Reproduction

On a project that originally received v0.31.0 from `DEFT.directive[bot]`:

```
$ ls -la .deft/
.deft/VERSION             # populated at v0.31.0 install time:
                          #   ref: 'v0.31.0'
                          #   sha: 'd4257b4...'
                          #   fetched_by: 'DEFT.directive[bot]'
.deft/core/               # framework source tree
.deft/core/VERSION        # ABSENT (not created at v0.31.0 install)
```

After `task deft:install:upgrade` (which wraps `run upgrade` → `_write_install_manifest`):

```
$ ls -la .deft/
.deft/VERSION             # STILL there with v0.31.0 contents — STALE
.deft/core/VERSION        # NEW — written by _write_install_manifest
                          #   ref: 'v0.33.0'
                          #   sha: '<wrong, see related issue>'
```

Now there are **two VERSION files with conflicting contents**, and the framework reads only the new one. The doctor's `manifest-agreement` check (output: `"detail": "Neither YAML manifest nor bare .deft-version exists; nothing to reconcile (greenfield install)."` against the OLD v0.31.0 state) confirms the doctor was already pointed at `.deft/core/VERSION` even while the actual install had the file at `.deft/VERSION` — so the drift was silent.

## Why this matters

1. **Operators don't know which file is authoritative.** A grep for the framework version returns both files. The stale one tells the truth about which release bot deposited the original install. The new one (when correct) tells the truth about which release the framework has been upgraded to. They look identical structurally and there's no header banner saying "deprecated location, see X".
2. **`framework_doctor.py` silently fails to flag the legacy file.** A check that inspects only the new location can't detect that the old file is also present. A consumer running `task deft:doctor` after upgrade sees a clean manifest-agreement result while the legacy file lurks.
3. **Tooling that reads `.deft/VERSION` directly (e.g. anything that DEFT.directive[bot] writes, or consumer-side automation that grep'd the version from there) silently keeps reading the stale value.** Result: bot-driven workflows that assume the bot-written `.deft/VERSION` is authoritative will lag behind real upgrades indefinitely.

## Expected behavior

One of the following:

**Option A — declare one location canonical and delete the other on upgrade.**

`task deft:install:upgrade` should:

1. Detect the presence of legacy `.deft/VERSION` (relative to the project root).
2. Either delete it (with a `BACKUP <path> -> <path>.premigrate` similar to `task migrate:vbrief` safety affordances) OR rewrite it as a deprecation-redirect stub:

```
# deft:deprecated-location-redirect
# This file's content moved to .deft/core/VERSION at framework v0.32+.
# See <link> for migration details.
```

3. Surface the action in the upgrade output: `"Migrated install manifest from .deft/VERSION → .deft/core/VERSION"`.

**Option B — support both locations and document the precedence.**

If keeping `.deft/VERSION` was a deliberate choice (e.g. so bots can write a parent-level file without descending into the install dir), document that explicitly and have `_write_install_manifest` write to BOTH locations on every upgrade so they never drift apart.

**Option C — at minimum, doctor must detect drift.**

`framework_doctor.py::manifest-agreement` should also check the legacy `.deft/VERSION` location and fail if both files exist AND disagree, with a fix pointer like "Run `task install:reconcile-manifest`".

## Bonus: bot writes are not part of the upgrade contract

The original v0.31.0 install was bot-driven (`fetched_by: 'DEFT.directive[bot]'`). Whatever pipeline the bot uses to populate `.deft/VERSION` is presumably separate from the `run upgrade` path. After the upgrade-by-clone in this consumer:

- `run upgrade` wrote `.deft/core/VERSION` (new location).
- The bot's `.deft/VERSION` (old location) is still there with v0.31.0 content.
- Next bot run might overwrite `.deft/VERSION` again with a newer version, but won't touch `.deft/core/VERSION`.

This means bot-driven and `run upgrade`-driven flows write to DIFFERENT files. They need to be reconciled.

## Suggested fix shape

In `cmd_upgrade`:

```python
# After _write_install_manifest writes <install>/VERSION:
legacy_manifest = project_root / ".deft" / "VERSION"
if legacy_manifest.is_file() and legacy_manifest != install_dir / "VERSION":
    # Backup, then redirect-stub or delete.
    backup_path = legacy_manifest.with_suffix(".premigrate")
    legacy_manifest.rename(backup_path)
    info(
        f"Moved legacy install manifest from {legacy_manifest} → "
        f"{install_dir / 'VERSION'}. Backup at {backup_path}."
    )
```

Add a regression test that:

1. Creates a project with both `.deft/VERSION` (v0.31.0) and `.deft/core/` (v0.33.0).
2. Runs `cmd_upgrade`.
3. Asserts `.deft/VERSION` is moved to `.deft/VERSION.premigrate` (or rewritten as a deprecation-redirect stub).
4. Asserts `.deft/core/VERSION` has the v0.33.0 contents.

And update the bot pipeline (`DEFT.directive[bot]`) so its future writes go to `.deft/core/VERSION` (or whichever location is declared canonical), not to `.deft/VERSION`.

## Environment

- Consumer: `deftai/statusreport`, branch `deftai-upgrade-20260518`
- Original v0.31.0 install: `.deft/VERSION` (parent-level)
- v0.33.0 upgrade target: `.deft/core/VERSION` (install-dir level)
- OS: Windows 11, pwsh 7.6.1
- Workaround applied during this upgrade: manually deleted `.deft/VERSION` after `task deft:install:upgrade` to remove the stale conflicting source

## Related

- #1046 PR-B AC-4 (install-manifest contract)
- #1062 (install_root field — implies install-manifest lives inside the install dir, but didn't migrate existing parent-level files)
- Resolver-vendored-installs issue (parallel filing — same metadata-canonicalization root family)
- Manifest-wrong-sha issue (parallel filing — compounds the problem by making the new location's content unreliable)

### 2026-06-03-1332-v0330-canonical-plain-file-installs-resolve-consumer-repo-ve: v0.33.0 canonical plain-file installs resolve consumer repo version and false-positive skill redirect stubs  `[completed]`

## Summary

When upgrading Appcellerator's canonical plain-file \.deft/core deposit from v0.32.1 to v0.33.0, the consumer install becomes unhealthy even though the install manifest and .deft-release-version are present.

## Observed

- \.deft/core/run version reports the parent consumer repo tag (0.0.1-alpha1) instead of Directive v0.33.0.
- \.deft/core/run gate reports NEEDS-UPGRADE recorded=0.33.0 current=0.0.1-alpha1.
- framework:check-updates resolves upstream_url to the consumer repo origin instead of https://github.com/deftai/directive.git.
- task upgrade rewrites \.deft/core/VERSION with the consumer repo HEAD sha because _resolve_framework_sha no longer reads the manifest first.
- run doctor reports real skill files as deprecation redirect stubs when those files merely contain the literal <!-- deft:deprecated-redirect --> in instructional prose.

## Expected

Canonical plain-file installs should resolve VERSION/ref/sha/upstream from \.deft/core/.deft-release-version and/or \.deft/core/VERSION when no nested Directive .git directory exists. Redirect-stub detection should only classify actual redirect stubs, not any full skill file that mentions the sentinel.

## Repro context

- Consumer: 3Ci-Consulting/appcellerator
- Install layout: .deft/core plain-file deposit, no nested .git
- Upgrade target: Directive v0.33.0, commit 0fc35e8749e88dfb648ff383501c4da3f5f344aa

### 2026-06-03-1389-doctor-agents-refresh-plan-interim-stub-emits-spurious-unrea: doctor: _agents_refresh_plan interim stub emits spurious 'unreadable' AGENTS.md-freshness warning on every consumer task doctor run  `[completed]`

## Context

During the Epic-1 doctor carve (#1335 / #1336), `scripts/doctor.py` became the owner of doctor core logic, ported from the retired `framework_doctor.py`. That port left `_agents_refresh_plan` as an **interim stub** because the AGENTS.md managed-section freshness helpers it depends on still live in the `run` module and have not yet been extracted to a shared module.

This was surfaced during review of #1380 (doctor extract/retire) and #1384 (installer-doctor handoff integrator) and intentionally **deferred as a tracked follow-up** rather than band-aided on the integrator branch.

## Current behavior

`_agents_refresh_plan` (`scripts/doctor.py`, ~line 1093) returns `{"state": "unreadable"}` unconditionally on consumer projects. `_run_agents_md_freshness_check` converts that into a **single warning per `task doctor` run**.

Because it is a warning (not an error), doctor's overall exit code is unaffected and CI stays green — but every consumer sees a spurious "unreadable" freshness warning on **every** doctor invocation, even when the AGENTS.md managed section is present, readable, and fresh.

## Expected behavior

`task doctor` should NOT emit an AGENTS.md-freshness warning when the managed section is actually readable and fresh. The freshness check should compute a real verdict (fresh / stale / genuinely-unreadable) instead of always reporting `unreadable`.

## Root cause

`_agents_refresh_plan` depends on AGENTS.md managed-section helpers (managed-section parse + sha + refresh-plan logic) that currently live in the `run` module. After the carve, the doctor module cannot import them cleanly, so the stub returns a placeholder verdict.

## Proposed fix

1. Extract the AGENTS.md managed-section / refresh-plan helpers from the `run` module into a shared module (e.g. `scripts/_agents_md.py`).
2. Have both `run` and `scripts/doctor.py` import from the shared module.
3. Replace the `_agents_refresh_plan` stub with the real implementation that returns fresh / stale / unreadable based on the actual managed-section sha + freshness probe (mirrors the session-start `task doctor` managed-section freshness probe — see AGENTS.md `## Template propagation discipline (#1309)` and session-start ritual step 2, #1308).
4. Add unit tests covering fresh, stale, and genuinely-unreadable states.

## Acceptance criteria

- `task doctor` on a consumer project with a fresh, readable AGENTS.md managed section emits NO freshness warning.
- A genuinely stale managed section still points the operator at `task agents:refresh`.
- A genuinely unreadable / missing AGENTS.md still warns.
- `_agents_refresh_plan` no longer returns a hard-coded `{"state": "unreadable"}`.
- Unit tests cover all three states.

## References

- Introduced by the doctor carve in #1335 / #1336 (PR #1380).
- Surfaced and deferred during PR #1384 (integrator) review.
- Related: AGENTS.md `## Template propagation discipline (#1309)`, session-start ritual step 2 `task doctor` (#1308).
- Files: `scripts/doctor.py` — `_agents_refresh_plan` (~line 1093), `_run_agents_md_freshness_check`.

**Acceptance**:

- Extract AGENTS.md managed-section / refresh-plan helpers from the run module into a shared module (e.g. scripts/_agents_md.py) `[proposed]`
- Have both run and scripts/doctor.py import the shared helpers (no duplicated logic) `[proposed]`
- Replace the _agents_refresh_plan stub with a real fresh/stale/unreadable verdict from the managed-section sha + freshness probe `[proposed]`
- task doctor on a fresh readable AGENTS.md emits NO freshness warning; stale points at task agents:refresh; genuinely unreadable/missing still warns `[proposed]`
- Unit tests cover fresh, stale, and genuinely-unreadable states `[proposed]`

### 2026-06-03-1420-triagereject-fails-when-triage-rejected-label-does-not-exist: triage:reject fails when 'triage-rejected' label does not exist on the repository  `[completed]`

When running 	ask triage:reject, the script unconditionally does gh issue edit --add-label triage-rejected after closing the issue.

If the label does not exist on the repo, the command fails with 'triage-rejected not found', causing the whole reject action (including audit rollback) to fail even though the issue was successfully closed with a reason.

Root cause: scripts/triage_actions.py hardcodes REJECTED_LABEL = 'triage-rejected' and has no logic to create the label if missing. No triage bootstrap or onboarding step ensures the required labels exist.

Expected: The system should either auto-create the 	riage-rejected label (and any other triage labels it depends on) or handle their absence gracefully without rolling back the decision.

See scripts/triage_actions.py:122 and the reject() function around lines 467-477.

### 2026-06-03-1424-cache-fresh-batch-eval: fix(triage): batch verify:cache-fresh scope filter so milestone is-open fetches once, not per-issue (#1424)  `[completed]`

## Problem

`task verify:cache-fresh` (script `scripts/preflight_cache.py`) takes ~92s on a 500-entry cache because its subscription filter calls `triage_scope.evaluate_rules()` once per cached issue instead of once for the whole batch. When the active `plan.policy.triageScope[]` contains a `{"rule": "milestone", "is-open": true}` rule, each per-issue call re-fetches the upstream open-milestones snapshot, producing an N+1 network fetch (~500 gh round-trips). The gate runs in the session-start ritual (#1149 step 5) and in the pre-`start_agent` gate stack, so the cost is paid on every interactive session start and before every implementation dispatch.

## Root cause

`_filter_scoped_meta_paths` loops over every cached entry and calls `_issue_in_scope -> evaluate_rules(rules, [issue])` with a single-issue list. `evaluate_rules` builds a fresh milestone resolver per call (memoized only within one call), so the `is-open` rule re-fetches the open-milestones snapshot once per issue.

## Proposed fix (Option A -- batch, mirror compute_drift)

Rework `_filter_scoped_meta_paths` to evaluate the rule set ONCE over the whole cache, matching `scripts/triage_scope_drift.py::compute_drift`: read every raw.json once into a list of issue dicts plus a `id(issue) -> meta_path` map; call `evaluate_rules(rules, all_issues)` a single time; keep each meta_path whose issue dict is in the returned matched set, resolved by OBJECT IDENTITY (not issue number, which can collide / drop None-number entries); preserve the over-include behavior for entries whose raw.json is missing/unparseable. This collapses N milestone fetches to 1, taking the gate from ~92s to well under a second with unchanged semantics.

## Acceptance criteria

- [ ] `_filter_scoped_meta_paths` calls `evaluate_rules` exactly once over the whole cache.
- [ ] A `milestone {is-open: true}` scope rule fetches the open-milestones snapshot at most once for an N-entry cache.
- [ ] Matched meta_paths are resolved by object identity, not issue number.
- [ ] Entries with missing/unparseable raw.json are still retained (over-include).
- [ ] Regression test in `tests/cli/test_preflight_cache.py` injects a counting fetcher and asserts at-most-once invocation, baseline equality, and missing-raw retention.

## References

- `scripts/preflight_cache.py` (`_filter_scoped_meta_paths`, `_issue_in_scope`)
- `scripts/triage_scope.py` (`evaluate_rules`)
- `scripts/triage_scope_drift.py` (`compute_drift` -- correct fetch-once reference)
- `scripts/_triage_scope_milestone.py` (`make_open_milestones_resolver`, `default_open_milestones_fetcher`)

**Acceptance**:

- _filter_scoped_meta_paths evaluates the rule set once over the whole cache (single evaluate_rules call). `[proposed]`
- A milestone is-open scope rule fetches the open-milestones snapshot at most once for an N-entry cache. `[proposed]`
- Matched meta_paths are resolved by object identity, not issue number; missing/unparseable raw.json entries are retained. `[proposed]`
- Regression test asserts at-most-once fetcher invocation, batched-equals-baseline matched set, and missing-raw retention. `[proposed]`

### 2026-06-03-1437-v0393-vendored-upgrade-leaves-root-artifacts-stale-duplicate: v0.39.3 vendored upgrade leaves root artifacts stale: duplicate AGENTS.md managed-section (attributed v3 marker not matched) + bare .deft-version not regenerated  `[completed]`

## Summary

`deft-install v0.39.3 --yes --upgrade --repo-root .` on a vendored (no-`.git`) install now **succeeds** (the #1433 `pax_global_header` extractor bug is fixed and the payload refreshes to v0.39.3 with an atomic backup). But the run leaves the install in a **doctor-failing state**: the vendored file-swap refreshes everything under `.deft/core/` yet does not correctly update the **two project-root artifacts derived from it**:

- **A.** the root `AGENTS.md` managed section is **appended (duplicated)** instead of rewritten, and
- **B.** the bare `vbrief/.deft-version` derivative is **not regenerated**.

Post-install `doctor --session` reports `errors: 1, warnings: 2` (`agents-md-managed-section-fresh: unreadable`, `manifest-agreement: fail`). Both are filed together because they share one root behavior: the file-swap upgrade only owns `.deft/core/**` and skips the root-level derived artifacts.

Reproduced on a webinstaller-origin vendored install (`deftai/statusreport`) whose pre-existing `AGENTS.md` carries the **attributed** v3 marker.

## A. Duplicate `AGENTS.md` managed section

### Observed
After upgrade, `AGENTS.md` contains **two** managed sections:
```
L1   <!-- deft:managed-section v3 sha=6136b66c42c8 refreshed=2026-06-01T03:08:04Z session=d7bc893a5c2d -->   (old, retained)
L139 <!-- /deft:managed-section -->
L142 <!-- deft:managed-section v3 -->                                                                          (new, appended)
L296 <!-- /deft:managed-section -->
```
Doctor: `{"check": "agents-md-managed-section-fresh", "status": "unreadable", "message": "AGENTS.md freshness check could not run (state='unreadable')."}` — two managed sections defeat the freshness probe.

### Root cause -- `cmd/deft-install/setup.go` (@ v0.39.3)
The managed-section detection/rewrite keys on the **exact, attribute-less** marker string:
- **L43** `agentsMDSentinel = "<!-- deft:managed-section v3 -->"`
- **L476** `hasV3 := strings.Contains(s, agentsMDSentinel)`
- **L387-401** `agentsMDManagedSlice` and **L418-435** `rewriteAgentsMDBlock` both locate the block via `strings.Index(body, openMarker)` with that same bare string.

A previously-installed `AGENTS.md` whose open marker carries the **v3 provenance attributes** (`sha=`/`refreshed=`/`session=`) does **not** contain the substring `<!-- deft:managed-section v3 -->` (there is ` sha=...` between `v3` and `-->`). So `hasV3=false` (and `hasV2`/`hasLegacy` are also false), and `WriteAgentsMD` falls through to its final branch -- documented at **L450-451**: *"no deft sentinel at all -> append the layout-correct v3 body"* -- appending a second managed section.

This is self-inconsistent: the comment at **L52-57** states "#1046 PR-B AC-5 bumps the canonical marker to v3 **with refresh provenance attributes**", yet the sentinel constant + all matchers only recognize the attribute-less form. (The attributed marker is what `run agents:refresh` / the relocator emit.)

### Fix
Two parts:

1. **Recognize attributed markers.** Match the open marker by **prefix/regex** rather than exact string -- e.g. `regexp.MustCompile(`<!-- deft:managed-section v3[^>]*-->`)` -- in the `hasV3` probe, `agentsMDManagedSlice`, and `rewriteAgentsMDBlock`, so an attributed v3 marker is recognized and surgically rewritten in place.

2. **Remove ALL prior managed sections first, then re-emit one (self-heal).** The rewrite MUST delete **every** existing deft managed section -- each `<!-- deft:managed-section v2|v3[^>]*-->` ... `<!-- /deft:managed-section -->` pair -- not just the first, and then write a **single** canonical managed section (preserving operator prose outside the fences). This makes the fix self-healing: installs already broken by this bug (two or more managed sections, like the repro above) are **cleaned up on the next `--upgrade`** instead of leaving the duplicates behind or appending a third. A naive "rewrite the first match" fix would leave existing duplicates in place -- the rewrite must converge any number of managed sections (0, 1, or N) to exactly one.

Add regression fixtures: (a) an `AGENTS.md` whose marker is `<!-- deft:managed-section v3 sha=... refreshed=... session=... -->` is rewritten (single section); (b) an `AGENTS.md` that already contains **two** managed sections collapses to exactly one; (c) a clean single-section file stays single.

## B. Bare `vbrief/.deft-version` not regenerated

### Observed
```
.deft/core/VERSION  -> tag: 'v0.39.3'   (fetched_by: deft-install)   <- refreshed
vbrief/.deft-version -> 0.0.0-dev                                     <- stale
```
Doctor: `{"install_check": "manifest-agreement", "status": "fail", "message": "Drift detected: YAML manifest tag='0.39.3' does NOT agree with bare .deft-version='0.0.0-dev'. ... run \`task upgrade\` to regenerate the bare derivative from the manifest."}`

### Root cause
The vendored file-swap updates `.deft/core/VERSION` (the canonical YAML manifest) but does not regenerate the **bare** `vbrief/.deft-version` derivative at the project root. The doctor's own remediation (`task upgrade`) is exactly the regeneration step the upgrade should perform itself.

### Fix
Have the vendored refresh (`upgrade.go`) regenerate `vbrief/.deft-version` from the manifest as part of the swap, so a fresh `--upgrade` is self-consistent and doctor-clean without a manual follow-up. (Same family as #1323/#1324/#1325.)

## Acceptance criteria
- After `--upgrade` on a vendored install whose `AGENTS.md` carries an attributed v3 marker, `AGENTS.md` has **exactly one** managed section (rewritten in place; operator prose preserved).
- Given an `AGENTS.md` that already contains **multiple** deft managed sections (e.g. produced by this bug), `--upgrade` removes all of them and collapses to **exactly one** canonical managed section.
- After `--upgrade`, `vbrief/.deft-version` agrees with `.deft/core/VERSION`.
- Post-install `doctor --session` reports no `agents-md-managed-section-fresh` or `manifest-agreement` failures.
- Regression tests for the attributed-marker rewrite, the multi-section collapse (self-heal), and the bare-derivative regeneration.

## Related
- #1433 -- `pax_global_header` extraction bug (fixed in v0.39.3; this is the next defect surfaced once extraction succeeds).
- #1425 / #1428 -- vendored upgrade support + git-free file swap (this is follow-up correctness in that path).
- #1323 / #1324 / #1325 -- vendored-install version/manifest stamping cluster (Finding B belongs here).

Evidence: tested with `deft-install v0.39.3` on a vendored `.deft/core` (no `.git`); `setup.go` read at tag `v0.39.3`; doctor JSON from the installer's post-install `--session` handoff.

**Acceptance**:

- Bug A: match the AGENTS.md managed-section open marker by regex ('<!-- deft:managed-section v3[^>]*-->') in the hasV3 probe, agentsMDManagedSlice, and rewriteAgentsMDBlock (setup.go), so attributed v3 markers (sha=/refreshed=/session=) are recognized and rewritten in place `[proposed]`
- Bug A self-heal: the rewrite removes ALL existing deft managed sections (v2|v3, attributed or bare) and re-emits exactly one canonical section, preserving operator prose outside the fences; converges 0/1/N sections to exactly one `[proposed]`
- Bug B: the vendored refresh (upgrade.go) regenerates the bare vbrief/.deft-version derivative from the manifest during the file-swap so it agrees with .deft/core/VERSION `[proposed]`
- Unit regression tests: attributed-marker rewrite -> single section; a pre-existing two-section file collapses to one; a clean single-section file stays single; bare .deft-version regenerated to match the manifest `[proposed]`
- End-to-end smoke test: run the vendored upgrade against a realistic fixture (attributed v3 AGENTS.md marker + real-shaped tarball + stale bare .deft-version) and assert the doctor invariants hold (exactly one managed section AND .deft-version agrees with the manifest); fail on pre-fix code, pass after `[proposed]`

### 2026-06-03-1440-deft-core-guard-rejects-every-deft-install-upgrade-pr-instal: deft-core-guard rejects every deft-install --upgrade PR: installer-managed root files (AGENTS.md, .agents/, vbrief scaffolding, ...) counted as 'app'  `[completed]`

## Severity

**High / affects all users.** Every consumer who commits the vendored framework and gates PRs with `deft-core-guard` (#1430) will find that **a `deft-install --upgrade` can never land as a single green PR** — the guard fails 100% of upgrade PRs by construction. The upgrade tool and its own deposited guard are in direct contradiction.

## Summary

`deft-core-guard.yml` refuses any PR whose diff mixes `.deft/core/**` with non-framework paths. But `deft-install --upgrade` writes **machine-managed files outside `.deft/core/`** too (`AGENTS.md`, `.agents/**`, `.gitignore`, `.gitattributes`, `greptile.json`, `.github/codeql/codeql-config.yml`, `.github/workflows/deft-core-guard.yml`, `vbrief/` scaffolding + `vbrief/.deft-version`). The guard classifies those installer-owned root files as "app," so an upgrade PR always looks "mixed" and is rejected.

## Reproduction (live)

deftai/statusreport#7 — a real `deft-install v0.39.4 --yes --upgrade` PR. Its single commit changes `.deft/core/**` AND installer-managed root files, so `deft-core-guard` fails it.

## Root cause

The guard (deposited by `cmd/deft-install/deposit.go`, #1430) computes "app" as "anything not under `.deft/core/`":
```sh
core=$(printf '%s\n' "$changed" | grep -E '^\.deft/core/' || true)
app=$(printf '%s\n' "$changed" | grep -vE '^\.deft/core/' | grep -v '^$' || true)
if [ -n "$core" ] && [ -n "$app" ]; then
  echo "::error ...::This PR changes .deft/core/** AND non-framework files..."
  exit 1
fi
```
There is no exemption for the installer's **own** root deposits. Worse, the upgrade legitimately *must* touch some of these every run — e.g. `AGENTS.md` (managed-section rewrite) and `vbrief/.deft-version` (manifest reconcile) per #1437 — so even a "steady-state" upgrade trips the guard.

## Proposed fix (allowlist the installer-managed surface)

Fail only when `.deft/core/**` is mixed with paths that are **neither core nor installer-managed**. Subtract an allowlist from the `app` set:
- `AGENTS.md`, `.agents/**`
- `.gitattributes`, `.gitignore`, `greptile.json`
- `.github/codeql/codeql-config.yml`, `.github/workflows/deft-core-guard.yml`
- `vbrief/.deft-version`, `vbrief/vbrief.md`, `vbrief/schemas/**`, `vbrief/migration/**`, `vbrief/<lifecycle>/.gitkeep`

**Critical nuance — do NOT exempt consumer-authored vBRIEF content:** `vbrief/PROJECT-DEFINITION.vbrief.json` and `vbrief/**/*.vbrief.json` are the user's data; if those are mixed with a `.deft/core/**` change the guard SHOULD still fire. That separation is the whole point of #1430.

Keep the allowlist authoritative by having the **installer emit the set of paths it manages** (e.g. in the VERSION manifest or a sibling list the guard reads), so the guard and the installer never drift. If a manifest is too much for now, hardcode the allowlist in the guard template and add a test asserting it matches exactly what `deposit.go` / the installer writes.

### Alternatives (weaker)
- **Idempotent root writes on `--upgrade`** (only touch root files when content changes): reduces churn but `AGENTS.md` + `.deft-version` still change every upgrade, so it does not eliminate the mix.
- **Label/trailer escape hatch** the guard skips for upgrade PRs: relies on humans applying it.
- **Documented admin-merge override:** normalizes a permanently-red check; weakest.

## Acceptance criteria

- An upgrade-only PR (diff limited to `.deft/core/**` + the installer-managed allowlist) **passes** `deft-core-guard`.
- A PR mixing `.deft/core/**` with genuine app files **or** consumer vBRIEF content (`PROJECT-DEFINITION.vbrief.json`, `*.vbrief.json`) still **fails**.
- The allowlist is installer-emitted (or a test asserts the hardcoded guard allowlist matches exactly what the installer deposits).
- Regression fixtures for both the passing (upgrade-only) and failing (mixed-with-app) cases.

## Related

- #1430 — the `deft-core-guard` deposit (the guard this issue fixes).
- #1425 / #1428 — vendored upgrade support + git-free file swap (the upgrade path that writes both core and root files).
- #1437 — upgrade rewrites `AGENTS.md` + regenerates `vbrief/.deft-version`, i.e. why root files change on every upgrade.
